one_line_takeaway: Splitting jailbreak-response filtering into role-specialized cooperating LLM agents lets a small open-source model (LLaMA-2-13b) defend a larger victim LLM, cutting GPT-3.5's jailbreak success rate from ~56% to ~8% while keeping false refusals low.
key_finding: A three-agent AutoDefense powered by LLaMA-2-13b reduces the Attack Success Rate on GPT-3.5 from 55.74% (no defense) to 7.95% on the DAN dataset, with 92.91% overall accuracy and a 6.76% FPR, outperforming all compared baselines including Llama Guard (Prompt+Response) at 21.28%, System-Mode Self-Reminder at 22.31%, Self Defense at 43.64%, and the OpenAI Moderation API at 53.79%. Adding Llama Guard as a fourth agent cuts the FPR of a LLaMA-2-7b defense from 37.32% to 6.80% while keeping ASR at 11.08%.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AutoDefense, a multi-agent LLM defense framework that mitigates jailbreak attacks via a response-filtering mechanism: instead of altering the user's prompt, it inspects each response the victim LLM produces and overrides it with an explicit refusal if judged harmful. The novel element is decomposing the harmfulness-judgment task into sub-tasks distributed across cooperating LLM agents (input agent, a "defense agency" of 1-3 agents, and an output agent), implemented on the AutoGen framework. The authors argue this decomposition lets small, weakly-steerable open-source models (notably LLaMA-2-13b) serve as effective defenders of a larger victim model, and that the framework can absorb existing defenses (e.g., Llama Guard) as additional agents. (Task decomposition, AutoGen, Llama Guard, Self-Defense, IAPrompt, and the jailbreak attacks themselves are cited prior work, NOT this paper's contribution.)
threat_model: Jailbreak attacks against safety-aligned LLMs. The attacker can manipulate only the prompt sent to the victim LLM and cannot directly access or modify the LLM's generated response; the primary failure mode targeted is "competing objectives" (instruction-following vs. harm-avoidance), instantiated via Refusal Suppression + Prefix Injection (Combination-1) plus AIM, Combination-2, and DeepInception attacks. The defender operates post-generation as a response filter, overriding harmful responses with a refusal, which makes the defense prompt-agnostic (robust across attack variations and victim models).
limitations: Authors' stated limitations (Appendix A.1): agents communicate in a fixed order rather than a dynamic, coordinator-decided topology; only a single agent role-assignment / prompt-decomposition strategy was designed; and only Llama Guard was tested as an integrated external defense component. Beyond these, the method's effectiveness hinges on the moral alignment of the chosen defense LLM — weakly-aligned models (Vicuna ~23-28% ASR, Mistral/Mixtral ~12-23% ASR) defend poorly, and small models like LLaMA-2-7b incur high FPR (up to 37-40%) without an added component. Being response-based, it doubles inference cost (generation then filtering), adding 2.81s (single) to 6.95s (three-agent) per response; the victim model is limited to GPT-3.5 (with Vicuna-13B and Mixtral-8x7B as secondary victims), and evaluation datasets are modest in scale.

datasets_used: Curated harmful set: 33 red-teaming prompts (from OpenAI/Anthropic efforts) expanded to 330 responses, includes PII-leakage cases
DAN harmful set: 390 questions across 13 forbidden scenarios, expanded to 1950 responses
Regular safe set: 33 GPT-4-generated safe prompts expanded to 330 responses
Stanford Alpaca: 1000 instruction-following prompt-response pairs (regular prompts)
quantitative_results: ASR on GPT-3.5 (DAN): No Defense 55.74% -> Three-Agent LLaMA-2-13b 7.95%; Single-Agent 9.44%
Baseline ASRs (DAN): OpenAI Moderation API 53.79%, Self Defense 43.64%, System-Mode Self-Reminder 22.31%, Llama Guard Response-Only 29.44%, Llama Guard Prompt+Response 21.28%
Adding Llama Guard as 4th agent (LLaMA-2-7b base): FPR 37.32% -> 6.80%, ASR 11.08% (three-agent baseline ASR 3.13%, FPR 37.32%)
Cross-setting (Table 2): Combine-1+GPT-3.5 Three-Agent 7.95% vs Self Defense 43.61%, Llama Guard(P+R) 21.28%, Self-Reminder 22.31%; AIM+Vicuna-13B Three-Agent 5.38% vs Self Defense 52.31%
DeepInception attack on GPT-3.5: ASR 36% -> 2% with LLaMA-2-13b three-agent defense
Defense latency (LLaMA-2-13b, H100, INT8): Single-Agent 2.81s, Two-Agent 5.53s, Three-Agent 6.95s
Undefended attack ASR on GPT-3.5 (Table 6): Combination-1 55.74%, Prefix Injection 34.36%, Refusal Suppression 29.74%, AIM 0.00%
baselines_compared: Single-Agent CoT defense (ablation within the framework)
